metadata.title: Agentic Research Course
metadata.author: Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.
metadata.description: Week 5: Literature Search and Review -- The 5-Stage Pipeline, Citation Grounding, and the Boomerang

elements.content: # Agentic Research Course
Week 5: Literature Search and Review

**Seyed Yahya Shirazi, Ph.D.**
Assistant Project Scientist, Swartz Center for Computational Neuroscience
UC San Diego

[Open Science Collective](https://osc.earth)
Course: [courses.osc.earth/agentic-research](https://courses.osc.earth/agentic-research/) | Discord: [discord.gg/3JtBrU3c](https://discord.gg/3JtBrU3c) | Recording: published to YouTube within 48 h

slides.speakerNotes:
- Welcome back

- Last week: CI/CD wired automated checks outside the repo

- Today: same checks-and-balances rigor applied to research output, starting with literature

- Format: ~30 min presentation, then ~5 min live demo on the practicum, then 15 min Q&A

- Course site, Discord, and recording note on the slide

elements.content: ## Where We Are

items.text: **Week 1 -- Git and GitHub.** The safety net.
**Week 2 -- Claude Code.** The agent.
**Week 3 -- Project management.** Epic, sub-issues, worktrees, plan mode, `/review-pr`.
**Week 4 -- CI/CD.** Automated checks outside the repo, on every push.
**Today -- Literature review.** The same rigor applied to research output. First pure-research week.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Each week's structure compounds. Week 5 is the first time we apply that structure to the research artefact itself.

- We are not adding new infrastructure today. We are pointing the existing infrastructure at a research output.

elements.content: ## Three Failure Modes of Unstructured Reviews

elements.alt: Three failure-mode icons: string-of-pearls, recency-bias clock, and a ghostly hallucinated paper

elements.content: Manual reviews already fall into these traps. **AI accelerates each by an order of magnitude** -- agents will fabricate plausible citations unless forced to ground claims in a corpus they have actually read.

slides.speakerNotes:
- String-of-pearls: one paper -> next paper -> next paper, no thematic argument. Looks well-cited from a distance, has no spine.

- Recency bias: the top of the search-result page becomes the field. The actual canonical work is below the fold.

- Hallucinated references: plausible author + year + DOI that does not exist. Most catastrophic in agent-driven workflows.

- The fix is not 'ask the agent to cite better.' The fix is structural -- a pipeline where every claim is mechanically traceable.

elements.content: ## AI Raises the Stakes

items.text: An agent will fabricate a credible-sounding citation **unless it is forced to ground claims in a corpus it has actually read**.
Confirmation bias is mechanical: the agent paraphrases the first half of the search result and ignores the second.
Recency bias is mechanical: the top of the result page wins unless you sort otherwise.

elements.content: The fix is **structural**, not stylistic. A pipeline where every claim is mechanically traceable to a stored, retrievable artefact. The next 17 slides show what that pipeline looks like.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- Confirmation bias is *not* a personality flaw, it is the agent's path of least resistance. The corpus discipline is the only durable defence.

- Recency bias: the agent never sees citation counts unless you sort by them. `--sort citations` and `canonical` queries are the antidotes.

- Land the structural-not-stylistic line. The whole rest of the talk is about what that structure actually is.

elements.content: ## Reframe: A Lit Review Is a Managed Pipeline

items.text: Same shape as Week 3: epic with sub-issues, branches, pull requests, `/review-pr`.
**Plus** citation grounding at every step (the cite-the-card rule).
**Plus** a boomerang from review back to direction or collection when gaps surface.

elements.content: **Lit review = research with a Definition of Done.** The same way a feature branch is not 'done' until CI is green, a literature review is not 'done' until every claim points at a stored card and the review boomerang has run at least once.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- The whole talk in one line: the pipeline is the answer.

- Definition-of-Done is the framing. Software engineers know this; it transfers cleanly.

- The boomerang is the rigor signal. We come back to it on slide 16.

elements.content: ## The 5-Stage Pipeline

elements.alt: Five-stage horizontal pipeline (Direction, Collect, Synthesize, Draft, Review) with a boomerang arrow looping from Stage 5 back to Stages 1 and 2

elements.content: Order is enforced. Drafting before synthesising is a **structural error**, not a style choice. Each stage has its own tools; we cover them next.

slides.speakerNotes:
- The hero diagram. Read each stage label aloud.

- Direction -> Collect -> Synthesize -> Draft -> Review.

- The boomerang arrow is the rigor signal. A review that never updates the corpus is hiding what it found.

- Worked example throughout: Annotation Garden Initiative (AGI) research foundation epic at github.com/Annotation-Garden/management/issues/3 -- 99 paper-cards, 5 synthesis docs, 3 direction papers.

elements.content: ## Stage 1 -- Direction with /project:epic-dev

elements.alt: An epic node fanning into four parallel strand sub-issues, each with its own collection folder

items.text: Plan-mode the topic into an epic + parallel **strand** sub-issues.
Strands are concurrent collection passes. Each strand: own brief, own branch, own PR.
Real example: AGI epic `Annotation-Garden/management#3`. Phase 1 collection across tools / data / science strands; ~99 cards.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 3x to reveal fragments]

- The Week 3 pattern, applied to research output instead of code.

- Strands are horizontally parallel. The point is to scope before searching, not to subdivide labour after.

- AGI link: github.com/Annotation-Garden/management/issues/3 -- public, browseable, has the full epic / phase / sub-issue structure.

- Today's practicum demo uses four strands by analytical perspective: psychophysics, action, language, emotion.

elements.content: ## Strand Brief Anatomy

elements.alt: A document silhouette annotated with five labelled regions: goal, scope, per-entry deliverable, acceptance criteria, out-of-scope

items.text: **Goal** -- one sentence. The strand's main claim or coverage area.
**Scope** -- 3-5 sub-areas the strand covers.
**Per-entry deliverable** -- what every paper-card must contain.
**Acceptance criteria** -- how we know the strand is done.
**Out-of-scope** -- what *not* to chase.

elements.content: Without the brief, Stage 2 collects whatever the agent's first search surfaces. The brief is a **scope contract**, not a courtesy line.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 6x to reveal fragments]

- Five fields. Every brief, every strand. Worth standardising.

- Out-of-scope is the most under-used. It tells the agent which adjacent rabbit-hole *not* to chase.

- The AGI tools-strand brief at research/_briefs/strand-A-tools.md is the canonical example.

elements.content: ## Stage 2 -- Three Search Strategies with opencite

elements.alt: A query node fanning into three labelled paths: canonical, search, and cite

elements.code:
# Foundational, high-citation works
uvx opencite canonical "naturalistic stimuli EEG" --max 10

# Recent or specific
uvx opencite search "movie-watching EEG developmental" \
  --max 20 --sort citations

# Citation graph traversal
uvx opencite cite "10.1126/science.1089506" --direction both

elements.content: Two interfaces. **`/opencite:opencite` skill** drives the workflow with judgement (preferred). **`opencite` CLI** at `github.com/neuromechanist/opencite` is what the skill calls underneath. Install: `uv pip install opencite` or `uvx opencite`.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 2x to reveal fragments]

- canonical: foundational, high-citation. Antidote to recency bias.

- search: recent or specific. Combine with --sort date or --sort citations explicitly; don't trust default order.

- cite: traversal. --direction both walks both citing-this and cited-by-this.

- Skill vs CLI: the skill drives the *workflow* (decides which strategy, evaluates results, decides whether to download). The CLI is a primitive. Demo uses the skill; CLI commands shown for transparency.

elements.content: ## Batched Retrieval and Conversion

elements.alt: Folder tree from batch-fetch --convert: pdf/, markdown/, markdown/img/ with open-access and paywall badges on PDFs

elements.code:
uvx opencite search "naturalistic EEG" \
  --max 20 -f json -o results.json

uvx opencite batch-fetch \
  --from-json results.json \
  --convert -o ./papers \
  --summary report.json

elements.content: **License-aware archival.** Open-access PDFs commit. Paywalled PDFs do not. Markdown extraction always commits (research-fair-use). The `redistribution_ok` flag in `meta.json` is the single source of truth.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 2x to reveal fragments]

- batch-fetch is the time-saver. Search once -> json -> batch all PDFs in one go.

- --convert is what makes the markdown side appear. Without it, just PDFs.

- License-awareness is non-negotiable. Committing a paywalled PDF to a public repo is a copyright issue. Markdown extraction is generally fine for research notes; flag the case in `meta.json` notes if uncertain.

elements.content: ## The Paper-Card Schema

elements.alt: Annotated card.md page with YAML front-matter and six required sections highlighted

items.text: Folder per paper: `card.md`, `source.pdf` (if redistributable), `source.md` (always), `meta.json`.
**Front-matter** is queryable: slug, type, year, doi, license, modalities, tags, relevance, pdf_status.
**6 required sections.** TL;DR, Summary, Relevance, Notable details, Open questions, Citations.

elements.content: The schema is the **rigor discriminator**. With it, the corpus is queryable and uniform. Without it, every entry is a free-form note.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- The 6 sections are non-negotiable. Skip Open Questions and Phase 2 gap analysis becomes much harder.

- TL;DR captures the *thesis*, not the abstract opening. Force the agent to extract one.

- Relevance is paper-to-strand specific. Generic prose 'this is relevant because...' is not acceptable; the agent must cite specific mechanisms.

- Schema lives in the practicum at sessions/week-05/practicum/_schema/paper-card.md, mirrored from the AGI repo verbatim.

elements.content: ## Calibration Anchors

elements.alt: A horizontal low-medium-high scale with anchor-example chips and two histograms above showing healthy spread vs over-concentrated

items.text: **high** -- direct dependency of the strand's main claim.
**medium** -- standard work in scope, not the anchor.
**low** -- tangential or background context.
**Recalibrate** if more than ~40% of entries land at one level. The field has lost discriminative power.

elements.content: The **first** of three defences against thumb-on-scale. Cheap, mechanical, and applies to any rated field. Anchor examples are spelled out in `_schema/paper-card.md`.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- Calibration anchors are the cheapest defence against thumb-on-scale. No prompt engineering, no review process, just anchor examples in the schema.

- The 40% rule is empirical from the AGI corpus. When everything ends up 'high relevance', the field is doing nothing.

- Same logic applies to any rated field, not just relevance. Use it for `md_quality`, `agi_relevance`, etc.

- We come back to defences-against-thumb-on-scale on slide 17.

elements.content: ## Stage 3 -- Synthesise: Structure Before Prose

elements.alt: Five thumbnails of synthesis outputs: tool ontology, dataset hierarchy, science map, gap analysis (highlighted), scope diagram

items.text: AGI Phase 2 produced **five docs**: tool ontology, dataset hierarchy, science map, gap analysis, scope diagram.
**No prose review yet.** Tables, hierarchies, maps.
Cross-strand references emerge here: a tool used by a dataset, a method enabled by a standard.
**Gap analysis is the bridge to Stage 4.** It names the claims the prose review will defend.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- Stage 3 is the most-skipped stage. The temptation is to draft as soon as you have papers.

- Forcing yourself to produce structured tables first is what catches dataset-level patterns: same paper appearing in two strands, conflicting findings, coverage gaps.

- The gap-analysis row is the seed of every claim in Stage 4. If you cannot name a row, you do not have a claim.

- AGI synthesis docs at github.com/Annotation-Garden/management/tree/main/research/synthesis -- ~700 lines across the five docs.

elements.content: ## Stage 4a -- /manuscript:manuscript-formatting

items.text: **Mini-review** -- focused, citation-rich essay defending a thesis. Today's practicum.
**Scoping review** -- map the extent of literature on a topic; broad search, categorisation.
**Narrative review** -- selective, thematic synthesis with arguments.
**Systematic review** -- exhaustive, reproducible search with PRISMA protocol; out-of-scope today.
**IMRAD background** -- the introduction section of a manuscript or grant. Week 6 and Week 7 revisit this skill.

elements.content: Pick the structure first. Different review types have different writing rules; the formatting skill encodes them so you do not have to remember them at draft time.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 6x to reveal fragments]

- Five common review types. Pick before drafting.

- Today's practicum uses the mini-review structure: thesis up front, thematic sections, no methods or results, citation-rich.

- Systematic reviews are out of scope today; PRISMA flow diagrams, registered protocols, and risk-of-bias tooling are an extension, not the default.

- Week 6 (grants) and Week 7 (manuscripts) revisit the same skill for IMRAD.

elements.content: ## Stage 4b -- The Cite-the-Card Rule

elements.alt: A draft paragraph with arrows from each claim to a paper-card icon; one claim has no arrow and is struck through

items.text: **Every claim cites a paper-card by relative path.**
If the agent cannot link to a `card.md`, the claim does not appear in the draft.
**Thematic** over chronological organisation. Group by argument, not by year.
**Citation weaving:** integral, non-integral, synthesis, contrast.

elements.content: The **second** of three defences against thumb-on-scale. Hallucinated citations are mechanically impossible if every claim points to a stored card.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- This is the moral rule of Stage 4. Land it.

- Citation weaving:
  - Integral: 'Hasson and colleagues showed...'
  - Non-integral: '...has been shown in naturalistic viewing [card]'
  - Synthesis: 'multiple lines of evidence converge on...'
  - Contrast: 'X reports A, while Y reports the opposite'

- Real example: the AGI direction-papers/science-direction.md has ~30 cite-the-card links across ~160 lines.

elements.content: ## Stage 5 -- /manuscript:paper-review and the Boomerang

elements.alt: The 5-stage pipeline with a thick boomerang arrow looping from Stage 5 back to Stage 2 and a thinner arc to Stage 1, with severity-tagged finding chips along the arrow

items.text: Plays the **peer-reviewer** role: methodological rigor, statistical validity, balance, scope creep.
Findings tagged by severity: **critical**, **major**, **minor**.
Critical / major findings cycle back: missing evidence -> Stage 2; mis-scoped direction -> Stage 1.
**Convergence in one shot is a red flag**, not a green flag.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- The peer-review skill is independent of the writing skill. Different agent, different prompt, deliberate adversarial framing.

- Severity tagging matters. Without it, all findings look equally important and you lose signal.

- The boomerang is the rigor signal. A first-draft review that passes everything either means the review is shallow or the corpus is hiding what it found. Either way, run again.

- Land the convergence-as-red-flag line.

elements.content: ## Bias and Rigor -- Three Defences

elements.alt: Three labelled shields: Calibration anchors (Stage 2), Cite-the-card (Stage 4), Boomerang (Stage 5)

items.text: **Calibration anchors** -- at scoring time. Cheap and mechanical.
**Cite-the-card** -- at drafting time. Makes hallucination structurally impossible.
**Boomerang** -- at review time. Cycles the corpus, not the prose.
Three defences, one per stage. Stack-redundant by design.

elements.content: **A review that converges in one shot has either nothing to say or is hiding what it found.** Favorable and unfavorable findings should both land. The defences exist to make that mechanically possible.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 5x to reveal fragments]

- This is the moral of the session. Land the punchline carefully.

- The three defences are stack-redundant: each catches a different failure mode at a different stage. None alone is sufficient.

- Bias-irrespective: a review of a hypothesis you wanted to confirm should still surface findings against it. Calibration anchors prevent thumb-on-scale at scoring; cite-the-card at drafting; boomerang at review.

elements.content: ## Live Demo Roadmap

items.text: **Topic.** Neural correlates of naturalistic movie watching, organised by four perspectives: psychophysics, action, language, emotion. Extends Week 3 HBN movie-watching practicum.
**Pre-built.** Epic + 4 strand briefs + 4 anchor cards (1 per strand) + synthesis stub + draft stub. Live in `sessions/week-05/practicum/`.
**Live action 1.** Add one new paper-card via `/opencite:opencite`.
**Live action 2.** Weave that card into one new synthesis paragraph via `/manuscript:manuscript-writing`.
**Live action 3.** Run `/manuscript:paper-review` on that paragraph. Whatever surfaces, we walk through.

elements.content: We do not manufacture a gap. If a real one appears (a likely natural one given the Healthy Brain Network's developmental cohort is **age-effect coverage**), we walk the boomerang back to Stage 2 with a follow-on `opencite search` -- described aloud, not run on stage, to stay in the time box.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 6x to reveal fragments]

- Land the topic and the pre-built state. Audience needs to see this is real, not theatre.

- The four anchor cards are at sessions/week-05/practicum/collection/{psychophysics,action,language,emotion}/<anchor>/. Hasson 2004, Hasson 2008, Huth 2016, Saarimaki 2016.

- Demo paper to add live: chosen during dry-run. Pick a 2024-2026 open-access paper from one of the four strands, ideally HBN-adjacent.

- Bias-aware: we do not manufacture a gap. If the review surfaces age-effect / developmental coverage as the natural finding, we walk the boomerang and end the demo cleanly.

elements.content: ## What Today Gives You / What's Next

items.text: **Today.** A literature review where every claim points at a stored, retrievable paper, and reviewer feedback updates the corpus instead of being argued away.
**Today.** Three defences against thumb-on-scale, one per stage of the pipeline.
**Next, Week 6.** Grant proposals -- starts from a literature review like the one we just built.
**Next, Week 7.** Manuscripts -- consumes the cards and bibliography produced here.

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 4x to reveal fragments]

- Take-home in two sentences: defensible review, mechanically grounded.

- Week 6 inherits the corpus directly: a Specific Aims page is a literature review condensed into one page.

- Week 7 inherits the bibliography and the schema for IMRAD background sections.

elements.content: ## What We Have / What We Do Next

items.text: **Have**
- The 5-stage pipeline (direction, collect, synthesize, draft, review).
- The AGI canonical example at `Annotation-Garden/management#3`.
- The practicum scaffold with 4 strand briefs and 4 anchor cards.
**Do next, live**
- Add one paper via `/opencite:opencite`.
- Weave one paragraph via `/manuscript:manuscript-writing`.
- Review that paragraph via `/manuscript:paper-review`.
- Walk the boomerang.

elements.content: **Questions? Ask while opencite searches.**

slides.speakerNotes:
[Press right 10x to reveal fragments]

- Final slide before the demo. Read the Have column first, then walk the Do-next column slowly so the audience anticipates the live actions.

- The closing line ('Questions? Ask while opencite searches.') is the bridge into the demo and Q&A. It signals that latency is fine; the audience can interject during the run.
